The plan aimed to benefit both poor and wealthy communities by providing equal access to quality instruction, and it was particularly conducive to children’s future development, though some parents embraced it while young learners were less enthusiastic. Critics also feared it might put students in isolated regions at a disadvantage due to technical hurdles, yet ultimately it promised to bridge learning gaps between different backgrounds. Goddard’s work gained substantial traction when he moved to New Mexico, attracted by its remote valleys and the ideal climate for year-round rocket launches. A laboratory and test range were already established there, and a weather expert invited him to continue his mission in the region. Over his career, Goddard secured more than 200 patents in rocket technology, earned recognition from scientists worldwide, and received a prestigious award from the U.S. government. His innovations also significantly boosted the military strength of the United States, cementing his legacy as a visionary whose forty-nine-year plan—from initial conception to realized impact—transformed the modern world.

sectionA:
W: Hi, Mark! I missed you at the history department meeting last week. Were you on holiday?

M: No, I wish. I was actually in hospital with a nasty throat infection. Then, to make things worse, I slipped on ice outside the pharmacy and broke my wrist.

W: Oh, that's awful! I hope you're recovering. I had my own troubles – my laptop crashed, and I had to ask the manufacturer for repairs. Anyway, I just finished reading your sister's new book on military history.

M: Oh, you mean Dr. Evans? Yes, she's a well-known military historian. She spent months visiting soldiers' wives and mothers to collect personal stories.

W: That sounds fascinating. But I imagine writing such a book is challenging.

M: Absolutely. She says the hardest part is that she doesn't have much freedom for imagination – she has to combine fact with fiction carefully to make her readers believe in the human experiences behind the battles.
sectionB: Today we explore how design and psychology shape our memories. Take Coca-Cola: in the 1930s, the company popularized the modern image of Santa Claus in its ads, making him a global icon. Later, polar bear commercials appeared in the 1990s. But its most practical innovation was the contoured glass bottle, inspired by the shape of a cocoa seed; this unique design was introduced specifically to combat counterfeits. Interestingly, while positive branding often succeeds, our brains are wired to dwell on negative events—rejections, injuries, or losses—far more than positive ones. This negativity bias explains why a single bad experience can outweigh many good ones, and why designers sometimes leverage surprise or discomfort to make a product unforgettable. Understanding these mechanisms helps marketers craft messages that stick.
sectionC: Good morning. Today we’ll explore three distinct topics: the economy of a small island nation, the effects of parenting styles, and common interview mistakes. First, consider the island’s economy. It is mainly based on agriculture, with coffee being the country’s primary export crop. Tourism and mineral exports contribute very little, and most farmers live a poor life, unable to hire household help. Shifting to parenting, research shows that children who are constantly overindulged and have their wishes immediately satisfied often grow up lonely, dependent, and dissatisfied. A key mistake many parents make is their unwillingness to allow their children to experience disappointment—they give in too quickly. However, learning to handle disappointment early actually helps children cope with setbacks and builds their ability to endure hardships later in life. Finally, regarding job interviews, one frequent error candidates make is saying the wrong thing at the wrong time, often due to nervousness. Employers, on the other hand, are most impressed by candidates who take initiative in their work, even more than those with just skill or experience.

sectionA:
W: Have you seen the controversy around Emma Green's new book? It's been polarizing readers.

M: I heard it's been very favorably received by some, but others are offended. She argues that even vegetarians can harm the environment through imported foods and excessive packaging.

W: True, but critics say she neglects people's efforts in animal protection. She also insists vegetarians are harming the environment by ignoring public transport benefits.

M: Her arguments are actually quite modest compared to radical activists. But they are significant—they've sparked real debate.

W: Her proposal to tax meat would generate money for public health, but opponents say it would force poor people to change their diet.

M: Still, it would help protect the environment if implemented properly.

W: Shifting topics—I read a study on what makes people successful. It's not just talent.

M: I think it's having someone who has confidence in them—a mentor or supporter.

W: Absolutely. And a nurturing environment with mutual respect among colleagues is crucial too.

M: Yes, understanding leadership and cooperative teammates also matter, but mutual respect really drives success.
sectionB: M: Today, we're going to talk about two major groups of bats: megabats and microbats. Megabats, often called fruit bats, have large eyes and highly developed visual centers in their brains. Unlike microbats, which rely on echolocation to hunt insects, megabats use their keen eyesight and sense of smell to locate fruit and nectar. In fact, their vision is so acute that some species can navigate even under starlight. This adaptation—their big eyes and distinctive visual centers—likely evolved to support their particular lifestyle: feeding on stationary food sources at night. They don't need echolocation because their food doesn't move. And while microbats are thought to have a common ancestor with shrews, megabats may have a different evolutionary origin. So, when we ask how these fruit bats find their way, the answer is not by echolocation or moonlight, but by a combination of vision and smell. Their large eyes compensate not for a lack of vision—they see quite well—but for the need to function in dim conditions, helping them travel long distances to find fruiting trees and adapt to changing weather patterns.

In today’s lecture, we’ll examine how young children process television programs. Research shows that children under the age of six often fail to follow complex narratives because they lack the cognitive and memory skills required. They are not emotionally prepared to handle conflicts in a show, and they tend to focus more on vivid images than on the plot. That’s why it’s crucial for parents to choose appropriate programs for their children, rather than leaving the remote control in little hands. Instead of just letting them watch, adults should help their children understand the program’s plot by outlining it first. After viewing, the most effective strategy is to explain the message of the show to their children, rather than simply asking if they enjoyed it or what characters looked like. This helps bridge the gap between what children see and what they truly comprehend.
sectionC: Today I'd like to discuss three interesting research findings. First, a study on why many people keep their shoes on indoors. Most respondents said they simply never developed the habit of removing shoes, while others believed a little dirt does no harm. But the same study examined bacteria on shoes and found that a single shoe can harbor hundreds of thousands of bacteria, including those commonly found on public‑toilet floors. Moreover, the chemicals on shoe soles can degrade indoor air quality. Now, shifting to psychology, researchers have explored how we handle negative emotions. Their advice: instead of trying to suppress feelings, we should accept them as normal and then try to understand what messages they convey. This helps manage emotions effectively. Finally, let's turn to agriculture. To cope with climate change, scientists have focused on cultivating new varieties of crops and developing more effective pesticides. However, a recent report noted that the expansion of farmland in developing countries has been a key driver of increased food production. Yet agricultural scientists have faced criticism for not turning their focus to the needs of farmers in poorer countries. One nation that invested substantially in agricultural research quickly rose to become a leading grain producer, demonstrating the power of targeted funding. These studies remind us of diverse challenges.

sectionA:
W: I just finished an interview with James Miller. You know, the author of that children's series?

M: Oh, the one who writes for *The Weekly*? He's a staff writer there, isn't he?

W: Exactly. He also writes fiction on the side, but his main job is at the magazine. What surprised me is how he manages his four kids.

M: Four kids? That must be chaotic. Are they into his stories?

W: Not really. They're actually very curious and autonomous — they love exploring the woods near their house, making up their own adventures. He never pushes them to read his work.

M: So how does he support their writing?

W: He gives them lots of encouragement. He never edits their stories heavily; instead, he praises their ideas and lets them develop naturally.

M: That sounds like a good approach. I remember his wife was a big reader. Did she influence him?

W: She did. She once told me that her reading completely changed how she perceives the world. She began to see everyday things differently — the light, the trees, even people's expressions. That's what sparked James's own writing.

M: Fascinating. So it's a family of creators.

W: You know, I never thought my hobby would turn into a career. After college, I started making my own clothes because nothing in stores fit me properly.  
M: So you were always interested in fashion?  
W: Actually, it was more about frustration. I'm quite tall and slim, so ready-made clothes were either too short or too loose. I simply couldn't find clothes in my size.  
M: That makes sense. And your designs? I heard your classmates loved them.  
W: Yes, they kept asking me to make pieces for them. But when I tried to sell to local shops, they all said no. They thought handmade items were too expensive.  
M: That must have been discouraging.  
W: It was, but I kept going. Now I run my own online store, and it's doing well. I'm a successful entrepreneur in my own small way.  
M: So you don't plan to study fashion design formally?  
W: No, I'm focusing on improving my marketing strategy instead. That's where I need the most growth.
sectionB: In a recent breakthrough, scientists have utilized artificial intelligence to discover a powerful new antibiotic capable of killing a deadly strain of bacteria that had been resistant to all known treatments. This achievement addresses the ever-growing crisis of bacterial resistance to antibiotics, which has become a major threat to global health. The AI system was trained to analyze thousands of potential compounds, rapidly identifying those most likely to be effective against the resistant bacteria. Crucially, the AI can also predict whether a compound is toxic to human cells, thereby streamlining the drug development process without completely dispensing with experimental testing. By scanning vast chemical libraries, the AI can foresee which molecules might work, reducing the need for years of trial-and-error. This approach not only speeds up discovery but also combats the spread of superbugs. The researchers emphasize that while AI cannot replace all laboratory verification, its predictive power is a game-changer in the fight against antibiotic resistance.

For centuries, people have tried to detect lies through theorization—constructing elaborate psychological models. However, research suggests that generalized rules about liars often fail. In fact, most deceptive signals are not easy to detect; they're subtle and easily masked. Moreover, many lies are well intended, told to protect feelings rather than to harm. So how do we actually catch deceit? Studies show it's mostly by chance, not through any systematic observation or conversation strategy. While some claim objectivity, the truth is that casual encounters reveal more than structured interviews. What specific techniques work? Looking the speaker in the eye is commonly recommended, but it can be misleading. Listening carefully to the speaker’s words often provides better clues than visual cues. Measuring the speaker’s breathing rate is another method, though it requires equipment. Focusing on the speaker’s facial expressions remains popular, yet micro-expressions are fleeting. The key is combining multiple approaches rather than relying on any single sign.
sectionC: Today’s lecture covers three key areas: patient-doctor dynamics, team collaboration, and personal finance. First, many patients feel that doctors don’t treat them with due respect. However, research suggests that patients who appear submissive and grateful to doctors and nurses often receive better care. In fact, adopting a passive attitude can lead to more positive interactions. Second, consider effective teams. The most successful teams work together despite risks of failure. Their mutual understanding stems from a common goal, not necessarily from similar backgrounds. Such teams are typically characterized by diversity, and over time their connections strengthen, even after the team disbands. Finally, on saving and investing. Financial planners advise putting aside twenty percent of one’s earnings. This habit enables people to focus on long-term investments and cope with irrational emotions. When investments fail, those who succeed are the ones who persist rather than get discouraged. They also avoid the trap of trying to keep up with others, which often leads to poor financial decisions. By following these strategies, individuals can build wealth steadily and avoid common pitfalls.

sectionA:
W: Professor, I’m still at the very beginning—I haven’t actually started writing my dissertation yet.  
M: That’s alright. Have you settled on a topic?  
W: I was thinking about the impact of online learning on student engagement.  
M: To be honest, I don’t think that topic is viable as it stands. It’s far too broad. You’ll need to narrow it down to something more specific.  
W: I see. But I’m also struggling to find good sources.  
M: When using electronic resources, be very selective. Stick to reputable journals and avoid general websites. I can point you to a few useful databases.  
W: Thanks. So I should focus on a narrower topic first, then work on the references.  
M: Exactly. Don’t feel rushed—taking time to refine your topic will save you effort later.

W: Welcome back to "Health Talk." Today we have Dr. Mike Sullivan, author of several bestsellers on nutrition. Dr. Sullivan, your latest book has created quite a buzz.

M: Thank you. Yes, my latest book sold a million copies in its first month. I've actually translated 12 books in total, but my own work focuses on nutrition and wellness.

W: Why do you think this book resonates so strongly with readers?

M: I think the main reason is that over half of Americans are now overweight. They're looking for science-based, easy-to-follow advice that actually works.

W: So who exactly are you writing for?

M: My target audience is the general public, but I especially hope to reach those who are overweight and want a sustainable way to lose weight.

W: And what is your core message?

M: I recommend cutting carbohydrate intake significantly. A personalized diet can help, but the single most effective change for most people is reducing carbs.

W: Sounds practical. Thanks for joining us.

M: My pleasure.
sectionB: Recent research has shed new light on how the brain responds to stress. In a study involving mice, scientists discovered that when the animals were exposed to mild, short-term stress, the rate of their growth increased dramatically. Specifically, the production of new brain cells in the hippocampus doubled, leading to improved cognitive function. However, when the stress became chronic, the mice began to show signs of depression and became increasingly irritable and restless. This suggests that not all stress is harmful; moderate stress can actually stimulate brain growth. So why do we experience stress? According to psychologists, one key purpose is to make good sense of challenging situations. By reflecting on the causes of stress, we can learn to adapt and cope more effectively. Interestingly, the study also found that moderate stress can produce a surprising healing effect: it provides protection against mental illnesses like depression and anxiety. In the long run, it may actually strengthen our immunity rather than weaken it. So while chronic stress is dangerous, a little stress might be just what we need to stay mentally resilient.

Many managers today struggle with a hidden crisis. They often prioritize their own interests over those of their staff, and become overwhelmed by daily routines rather than focusing on strategic leadership. A common pitfall is spending excessive time handling email, which leaves little room for genuine connection with employees. As a result, their leadership may be challenged; if left unaddressed, major problems can emerge, and in the worst case, their companies may even face bankruptcy. So what should effective leaders do? First, they need to keep an eye on their employees’ well-being and actively motivate and inspire their team. This sometimes requires sacrificing some of the immediate goals for the sake of long‑term growth. Greater ambition in overall planning is also essential—leaders must look beyond the present. On a practical level, they should cultivate self‑control when it comes to digital distractions. Instead of constantly checking their inbox, they can filter their email boxes to prioritize important messages, respond only after work if necessary, or check only at set times during the day. By doing so, they free up mental space for more meaningful work and stronger leadership.
sectionC:
Today I'd like to discuss two interesting topics: the role of food appearance in consumer choices, and the surprising fate of coins thrown into fountains. First, consider how we decide what to eat. Research shows that focusing on nutrients alone is not the most effective way to promote healthy food. Instead, stressing the pleasing aesthetics of food—how it looks on the plate—can significantly boost its appeal. Marketing professors have found that when healthy foods are made visually attractive, consumers are more likely to choose them. This insight helps businesses increase sales of nutritious products. Meanwhile, the organic food industry has seen a spectacular surge in demand, with many small businesses offering environment-friendly options.

Now, let's turn to a different tradition: throwing coins into fountains. Around the world, this practice is a time-honored ritual. The coins are regularly collected by cleaners. What happens to them? In many cities, they are donated to charity organizations, helping fund community projects. However, not everyone respects this custom. Recently, a man was arrested for stealing a large amount of money from a fountain using a magnetic stick. His actions not only broke the law but also disrupted a cherished routine. So, while fountain coins can support good causes, they also attract dishonest individuals.

sectionA: Today I want to explore a common question: does more money mean more happiness? Many people assume a higher income leads to greater wellbeing, but research tells a different story. Take my friend Sarah. A few years ago she was deeply in debt, earning just thirty thousand dollars a month, yet she described herself as genuinely happy. She wasn’t a financial adviser; she simply realized that happiness doesn’t come from consuming more every time you earn more. Instead, she advocates prioritizing joy over money and even started a club to share the ideas from her new book. This raises an important point about what I call the “reference point” of happiness. Your sense of wellbeing isn’t a fixed measure of your earning power; it constantly shifts with your environment and your personal goals. For example, consider a man named John. If he chose to reduce his work hours, he would lose some income and might feel a loss of individuality. But the trade-off would give him more time with his loved ones—a benefit many people find invaluable. For those who value relationships over wealth, such a sacrifice can be deeply fulfilling. So next time you think about your finances, remember: money can buy comfort, but happiness often depends on what you choose to prioritize.

W: Did you see that new painting in the west wing? The one called "Silent Meadow"?

M: Yes, I was just looking at it. Beautiful, but I heard it's not the artist's first landscape—she did several earlier ones.

W: Actually, the placard says it was donated by the artist herself. It's not on loan or anything.

M: Interesting. I wonder who owns it now. Some anonymous collector, I suppose.

W: That's what I read. The gallery only recently acquired it as a donation.

M: The style is so expressive. It really seems to reflect her emotions rather than just depicting a scene.

W: Absolutely. There's almost no fine detail, and it's far from symmetrical. But I find the empty fields strangely beautiful.

M: Speaking of the artist, I met her once. She's not eccentric like you'd expect—actually very nice and intelligent.

W: And productive too. But I think her personality really comes through in this piece.

M: Agreed. It's a remarkable work.
sectionB: Forgiveness is often misunderstood as a sign of weakness, but research shows it is vital to one’s mental health, reducing stress and anxiety. However, forgiveness does not always come easily. It becomes particularly difficult when the offender has power over the victim—for instance, a boss or a parent—because the imbalance makes the victim feel trapped. Other barriers include the offender refusing to apologize or the offense being especially humiliating. Before extending forgiveness, it is crucial to determine how serious the offense was, as not all transgressions warrant immediate pardon. Only after carefully evaluating the harm can one decide whether to talk with the offender calmly or seek reconciliation.

W: Today we're going to talk about the city's metro system. Originally built in the 19th century, it underwent a major upgrade in the 1990s. The number of stations increased to over 50, making it the longest underground network in the United Kingdom. This expansion was intended to increase capacity to meet the growing needs of commuters. The metro is now an essential part of the city's transport, helping to reduce traffic congestion in the centre. Although not a world heritage site, it is a historic landmark. Modern features include high-tech systems for signaling and ticketing, and stations are generally well-maintained.
sectionC: Good morning. Today we'll explore three distinct topics. First, consider bears in national parks. Contrary to popular belief, bears are quite unafraid of humans and often wander near campsites. However, feeding them is strictly forbidden, as it creates dangerous habits. If you encounter a bear, never share your food, and always avoid teasing bears, especially mothers with cubs. Second, let's discuss prejudice. Prejudice means making judgments without adequate knowledge. These biases allow myths and half-truths to persist, preventing us from getting to the truth. Prejudice often develops when we live in isolated neighborhoods, cut off from diverse perspectives. Finally, think about childhood lessons. Research shows that values instilled in youth can last a lifetime. One teacher recalled that his own difficult experiences helped him become mature, and he saw his role not just as an instructor but as someone who could help his students through growing pains. That mindset shaped his entire career.

sectionA:
W: You know, I finally moved into my new apartment yesterday. It's so different from the dorm.

M: Really? How does it feel?

W: The dorm was so tiny and noisy—I could barely concentrate. But this place is spacious and quiet. I love it.

M: I had a similar feeling when I moved out of my old shared house. It was shabby and lonely at first, but eventually it felt like paradise.

W: Exactly! At first I missed the chaos of the dorm, even the people talking loudly. Now I don't mind it anymore. I actually kind of miss it.

M: That's interesting. So you've changed.

W: Yeah. But my new apartment is so cozy. I want to make it perfect.

M: I can help you decorate it if you like. I have some spare furniture.

W: That would be great! Want to come over and see it now?

M: Sure, let's go.

W: Welcome to our program, "Education Insight". Today we have Dr. Mark Green, who recently published a book titled *Reimagining the University*. Dr. Green, what inspired you to write it?

M: Thank you. I've spent years observing how universities operate, and I'm concerned that they are failing to prepare young people for the job market. The curriculum is often outdated, and graduates lack practical skills.

W: So you believe the system is broken. How should funding be reallocated?

M: Yes. Currently, engineering and technology get massive budgets while literature and arts are underfunded. I argue that literature should receive as much funding as engineering. Both are essential for a well-rounded education.

W: Some say the goal of higher education is to build a prosperous nation or create skilled professionals. What's your view?

M: Those are important, but I think the ultimate purpose is to cultivate better citizens—people who can think critically, empathize, and contribute to society in meaningful ways.
sectionB: Today, we’re exploring a mental phenomenon that most of us experience daily yet rarely stop to analyze. Psychologists call it “mind wandering,” and it is remarkably common—in fact, studies show that nearly half of our waking hours are spent with our attention drifting away from the task at hand. Yet because it occurs when we are alone, either physically or mentally, it often goes unnoticed by others. Contrary to popular belief, mind wandering seldom annoys people; many actually find it refreshing. What sets truly creative individuals apart is not just the ability to daydream, but a special understanding of how to harness this state. They know how to make their mental batteries work—consciously activating the brain’s default mode network to fuel innovation. So when do these flashes of insight occur? Not during intense activity, surprisingly, but rather when we are fantasizing in our down time—letting the mind roam freely during relaxation, whether staring out a window or taking a leisurely walk. This unstructured reflection is the fertile ground where original ideas take root.

Today, we’ll explore the early postal system in the American West. Before the Gold Rush, surprisingly, post offices already existed in many frontier towns, serving as vital communication hubs. Later, as gold seekers poured in, the postal service expanded significantly. Its primary role was not just economic—it kept people scattered across deserts and plains connected, reducing isolation. The federal government did not rely on Native Americans as postmen; instead, it commissioned private wagons and stagecoaches to carry the mail across vast distances. This approach was more efficient than subsidizing locals or centralizing services in remote areas. Recently, a historian examined this system not by reading books or studying maps manually, but by applying data science to historical trends, analyzing thousands of mail route records. His findings reveal how postal networks shaped settlement patterns and federal influence in the region.
sectionC:
Today’s lecture explores three fascinating areas of psychology and behavior. First, let’s consider anxiety and memory. Research shows that moderate anxiety can sharpen our recall—people with higher anxiety levels often remember threatening or emotional details better. However, when anxiety becomes extreme, it impairs cognitive performance, causing forgetfulness and confusion. In one study, researchers measured participants’ anxiety levels before showing them emotionally charged photos. Those with moderate anxiety recalled more details, while those with severe anxiety performed poorly.

Now, turn to consumer behavior in the digital age. Modern customers expect instant responses; they lose patience if their calls aren’t answered promptly. To engage them effectively, companies should speak directly to their emotions rather than just listing product features. Furthermore, businesses must keep up with the latest technological developments, as consumers quickly abandon brands that fall behind.

Finally, consider workplace friendships. Working together naturally strengthens bonds, and these friendships boost job satisfaction. Supportive friends at work also enhance our self‑esteem, making us feel valued and capable. In contrast, purely work‑based relationships without genuine friendship rarely provide these benefits.

sectionA:
W: I’ve always been fascinated by architecture. It’s not just the look of buildings—it’s how design and engineering work together perfectly.

M: So you’re drawn to that integration? Did your father, the architecture professor, influence you?

W: He did introduce me to the field, but what really hooked me is that blend of creativity and structure. I find it amazing how form and function merge.

M: I see. How did you manage to learn so much about it? Through formal courses?

W: Mostly through hard work—reading, sketching, and analyzing buildings on my own. I did take a few prerequisite courses, but self-study was key.

M: That’s impressive. Architecture is such a long-lasting field; the best structures stand for centuries.

W: Exactly. That’s part of its appeal. It’s not just temporary or immaterial—it endures.

M: Speaking of enduring interests, do you have any other subjects you enjoy besides architecture?

W: Yes, I’m really into Western art. The history and techniques influence my architectural ideas.

M: That makes sense. Art and architecture often go hand in hand.

W: Thank you for coming. We’re looking for a personal assistant for Mr. Sanchez – you may know he’s the famous football coach for Real Madrid, very well known to the public.  
M: Yes, I’ve seen him on TV. Does he get bothered by fans often?  
W: Occasionally, yes. And we need someone to manage his public profile and schedule.  
M: I should be honest – I have little previous work experience, but I’m a truthful person.  
W: Honesty is good, but what really matters is your ability to connect with people. You’ll need to handle media and fans.  
M: I’m good with people. So the job is to help promote his public image?  
W: Exactly. Your main role will be to serve as his personal assistant and boost his public profile.
sectionB: (lecture) Many parents today worry excessively about the physical risks involved in adventure sports for their children. While safety is certainly important, this overprotectiveness often does more harm than good. For one thing, adventure sports like rock climbing, kayaking, or mountain biking bring greater benefits to young people precisely because they require facing real challenges. When parents impose too many rules or pressures, they may actually make these activities less suitable for young people. More importantly, the constant safety rules and parental fears prevent kids from enjoying the very essence of adventure sports. By eliminating all perceived dangers, parents rob their children of the chance to cultivate courage—the kind that comes from managing risk rather than avoiding it entirely. Similarly, team-based adventure sports teach young people how to cooperate and support each other; if kids are never allowed to participate, they miss the opportunity to develop team spirit. However, this doesn't mean we should send children into dangerous situations unprepared. A better approach is to introduce them to these sports step by step, starting with controlled environments and gradually increasing challenge levels as they gain skill and confidence. We can also encourage them to take up these sports when they are more mature, but we should never completely deprive them of the valuable lessons that only adventure sports can teach.

Welcome to today's lecture on consumer electronics and sustainability. One common frustration among users is that their devices seem to stop working shortly after the warranty expires. This is no accident—tech firms intentionally design products with short lifespans, a practice known as planned obsolescence. While consumers may crave the latest model, the primary driver is manufacturers' strategy to encourage frequent upgrades. In response, France has introduced a new regulation requiring companies to list a repairability score for their products, rating how easily they can be fixed. This aims to empower consumers to make informed choices. However, the bigger issue is the growing mountain of electronic waste. To tackle this, we must take the initiative to reduce e-waste—by repairing devices, buying second-hand, and pressuring companies to design longer-lasting products. Some argue the problem will only worsen, but I believe it can be solved if all stakeholders act responsibly.
sectionC:
Today's lecture covers three distinct research topics. First, consider cyberloafing—employees using the internet for personal tasks during work hours. Many assume it indicates laziness, but studies reveal it can actually relieve stress and improve focus when taken as mini-breaks. Surprisingly, moderate cyberloafing is linked to higher productivity, not decline. However, excessive use correlates with lower engagement and higher turnover.

Next, let's turn to the history of Arbor Day. In the 1850s, Nebraska's landscape was treeless, leading to harsh environmental conditions. Julius Sterling Morton, a newspaper editor and later chairman of the state board of agriculture, promoted tree planting through his paper. His efforts led to the first Arbor Day in 1872, during which Nebraskans planted over a million trees. The state later earned the nickname "the Tree Planters State."

Finally, recent discoveries in Guangxi, China, specifically two modern human teeth found in Luna Cave, push back the timeline of hominid migration out of Africa. These fossils suggest early humans adapted to diverse environments, likely due to abundant local food sources. The findings indicate that human dispersal across Asia occurred much earlier than previously thought, challenging our understanding of migration patterns.

sectionA:
W: Good morning, Mr. Chen. Please take a seat. I’ve looked over your test results.

M: Good morning, Doctor. Is everything all right? I’ve been feeling tired and dizzy lately.

W: Well, your blood pressure is a bit elevated, and your circulation indicators show some irregularities. Nothing severe yet, but there are signs that your blood circulation may not be working as well as it should.

M: I thought it was just stress from work. I’ve been under a lot of pressure lately.

W: That’s a common mistake. Many people confuse physical symptoms with stress. Your body is trying to tell you something more than just being overworked. The issue is that patients often suffer from an illness without recognizing it until it gets serious.

M: So what should I do? Do I need more tests or medication?

W: I’m not going to prescribe medication right now. Instead, I think it’s more important for you to understand the common consequences of long-term stress and how they affect your body. I’ll explain them to you in detail.

M: I see. And what about my job? I’m in a very competitive field.

W: Your industry calls for responsible management—of your time, your health, and your team. It’s full of competition, so you need to manage stress carefully.

W: Have you been following the story of the retired basketball player, Kevin Wang?
M: The one who quit at the height of his fame? I heard he did it to avoid being in the limelight. Is that true?
W: Exactly. He couldn't stand the constant attention.
M: So what did he do next? I recall he worked as a financial advisor for a while.
W: That's right, but he didn't enjoy it. Then he fell ill and needed medical treatment.
M: I remember he was hospitalized. But after recovering, he returned to the sport as a coach, didn't he?
W: Yes, he became a basketball coach for a youth team. Actually, his career has had many ups and downs.
M: And now? I heard he's involved in a new project.
W: He's started a foundation to train young basketball players, especially from low-income families. He's also raising public funding for the program.
M: That's great. He's really making a difference.
sectionB: Today's lecture explores the career of acclaimed novelist Kate Atkinson. Born in 1951, Atkinson initially pursued academia. She began teaching at Edinburgh University in Scotland after completing her doctoral studies on American Literature? Actually, no. Her teaching career started later. It was only after winning the 1986 Woman’s Own Short Story Competition that she gained confidence to write fiction. However, her first novel, *Behind the Scenes at the Museum*, was published in 1995, long after she had started teaching. So when did she begin teaching? That was while she was still a doctoral student, not after the competition or the novel. Now, regarding her thematic concerns, Atkinson is known for exploring the code of human behaviour, especially the quirks and flaws that define us. Her characters often include eccentrics who defy social norms, yet they are not punished for their sins—instead, they are frequently rewarded or at least escape consequences. These individuals are not ignorant of complex human relations; rather, they understand them intimately. They do not imitate the manners of fashionable circles; they are often marginal figures. Thus, Atkinson’s work celebrates the flawed and the odd.

In today's lecture, we'll explore the concept of emotional intelligence within teams. Emotional intelligence, or EQ, refers to the ability to recognize and manage one's own emotions and those of others. It plays a pivotal role in group dynamics. For instance, team members use EQ to alleviate tension, ensuring conflicts do not escalate. This skill is highly sought after by modern employers, as it helps teams navigate recessions by fostering resilience. Moreover, EQ is essential when learning new tasks, as it facilitates open communication and feedback. Research indicates that individuals with high EQ tend to make better choices, as they can assess situations calmly and consider multiple perspectives. They are not merely following innovative ideas impulsively but rather evaluating them critically. In today's economy, the shift from manufacturing to service and knowledge industries means workers increasingly use their brains rather than muscles. Consequently, emotional intelligence becomes even more critical, as employees often work in flexible environments that require self-regulation. When faced with challenges, high-EQ groups do not abandon their goals; instead, they confidently stick to their original objectives while anticipating possible setbacks. They adjust priorities only when necessary, but they do not rush to leave the group. Understanding these dynamics can significantly improve team performance and individual career growth.
sectionC: Today, we explore three fascinating topics. First, what sets humans apart from animals? Our great cognitive capacity stems from creativity—not just for geniuses, but something every human can do. We use existing ideas to combine them into novel inventions, unlike animals that repeat precedent. Second, consider dogs’ remarkable abilities. They know when their owners aren’t feeling well, partly due to an extremely powerful sense of smell that can detect illness. This skill benefits owners both physically and mentally, as dogs alert them to health changes. Finally, let’s turn to a UK policy debate. The government plans to restrict the ability of creative arts courses to recruit new students, arguing that STEM degrees offer better returns. At first glance, this seems reasonable. However, critics point out that a high proportion of creative arts students earn less than £25,000 a year, but the real context is Britain’s economy as a whole, not just individual degree earnings. The policy overlooks how creative skills drive innovation and well-being.

sectionA:
W: I'm thinking about our summer holiday. Everyone's been talking about this new trend—renting a high-tech van instead of staying in resorts.

M: You mean like a camper van? I've heard about those. They're not just ordinary vans. They can generate their own electricity using solar panels, so you're never stuck without power.

W: Exactly! And they come with advanced navigation that receives instructions via computers, helping you avoid bad weather. That means you can drive under almost any condition, safely.

M: That sounds appealing. I never liked being locked into one place on vacation. With a van, you have total freedom. You can park near a mountain and go biking right from your doorstep.

W: Yes, and it's a great way to slow down in our increasingly hectic lives. You choose where to go and work remotely if you want. It's a novel way to spend the holidays.

M: I'm sold. Let's check out the best deals online.

W: I’ve been under a lot of mental pressure lately, and it’s really hurting my focus. My output has dropped, and I even missed a couple of appointments.

M: I’ve noticed your performance has declined significantly over the past month. You used to be so reliable. Is it just stress, or something physical?

W: Mostly my mind feels disturbed. I can’t concentrate, and I’m worried about being penalized for the curtailed output.

M: Forget about penalties. Health comes first. I think you need a break. Take the next two Mondays off. That should help you get back to your usual self.

W: Thank you for your whole-hearted support. I really appreciate it.

M: Your work proficiency has always been outstanding. You just need some time to recover. I’m sure you’ll be back at your best soon.
sectionB: Marriage has long been a subject of interest in health research. Studies show that being married can significantly affect our emotional state, often boosting mood and reducing stress. This emotional impact is one of the most direct ways marriage influences our well-being. Beyond emotions, researchers have discovered that a spouse's educational background plays a crucial role in determining an individual's health outcomes. In fact, the level of education a spouse has attained is a stronger predictor of one's health than the individual's own education or income. This suggests that health is not only shaped by personal choices but also by the social environment within a marriage. Furthermore, when examining couples who report the best health, a notable pattern emerges: they tend to have a high degree of similarity with their partners. These couples often share common interests, values, and educational backgrounds. The alignment in their life circumstances seems to foster mutual support and healthier lifestyles. Thus, while marriage itself can improve mood, the specific benefits for long-term health appear most pronounced when spouses share similar educational and social backgrounds.

W: Good morning. Today I’d like to share a recent urban flood research project. Our primary goal was not just to understand changing climate patterns or geological features, but to forecast flood risks with much greater accuracy. Traditional methods often fail to capture rapid flash floods, so we focused on real-time prediction. To achieve this, we deployed a network of Internet-connected water-level sensors along vulnerable streets and drainage channels. These sensors transmit data every minute to a central system. This allows us to monitor rising water levels instantly and issue warnings. Some might think we used video cameras or supercomputers, but the key innovation was the low-cost sensor network. Citizens can also access the data to prepare for incoming floods. The system has already proven effective in several test cities.
sectionC:
Today I want to discuss several interconnected trends shaping our society. First, consider the sharp decline in employment among men without college degrees. This isn't simply about job loss—it's about changing job requirements. Many positions that once offered stable wages now demand higher education, leaving less-educated men struggling. This has contributed to a dramatic drop in marriage rates among that group, as economic instability affects family formation.

Meanwhile, consumer attitudes are shifting in the fashion industry. More people now prioritize animal welfare when buying clothing, pressuring brands to avoid leather and fur. Some companies have even developed silk substitutes from mushrooms. Yet we must ask: do these changes truly represent substantial ethical progress, or are they merely symbolic?

Finally, despite widespread pessimism, data shows our era is actually the most peaceful in history. Why do so many believe the world is deteriorating? The answer lies in our psychological biases: we are wired to pay more attention to negative information and to vacuum up depressing stories. This negativity bias distorts our perception of reality, making us ignore genuine improvements. Understanding these biases helps us evaluate social change more objectively.

sectionA:
W: So, I heard you’ve been experimenting in the kitchen lately. What’s all the fuss about?

M: Well, I decided to do thorough research on the price of kitchen appliances before buying anything. I ended up reading numerous comments users put online about different blenders. Then I bought one and now I blend all my food—even things you wouldn’t expect.

W: That sounds a bit extreme. I could never get into eating any blended food. There’s something about the texture that puts me off.

M: It’s actually amazing. I’ve developed a habit of making my own fresh fruit juice regularly every morning. It’s become part of my routine, and I feel so much healthier.

W: I suppose that’s better than buying sugary drinks. But don’t you worry about the sugar content? I heard that even a fruit smoothie can have one-tenth of it as sugar.

M: True, but it looks healthy and attractive, and it contains an assortment of nutrients. Besides, one’s fancy may be tickled by it. I think it’s worth it.

W: I still prefer cooking every meal creatively in the kitchen. That way I know exactly what’s going into my food. But to each their own, right?

W: Mr. Mayor, thank you for joining us. Many say Berkton has undergone a remarkable transformation under your leadership. How did you manage to turn this once-sleepy village into a popular destination?

M: Well, it wasn't easy. When I first took office, Berkton was a relatively unknown place, with very limited development. The residents were used to a quiet, isolated life, but I saw potential. I started by convincing them that we could change the village radically, without losing its charm. We focused on preserving the unique aesthetic of the houses — in fact, all the properties in Berkton were designed by the same architect, which gives the village a cohesive, charming look. 

W: So the majority of residents now enjoy cosy housing conditions?

M: Exactly. And more importantly, these changes have helped boost the local economy. Tourists come for the architecture, which has contributed considerably to our popularity. The residents are proud, but the real joy is seeing how the village has come to life. Everyone here is happier now.
sectionB:
W: Welcome to Science Today. I'm joined by Dr. Chen from Northwestern University, whose team just published a breakthrough in robotics. Dr. Chen, tell us about your latest creation.

M: Thank you. We've developed the smallest remote-controlled walking robot ever built. It's only half a millimeter wide, yet it can bend, crawl, walk, turn, and even jump. Our findings appear in the latest issue of Science Robotics.

W: That's incredible. How does it move without conventional motors?

M: It doesn't use motors or complex hardware. Instead, the robot is powered by the elastic property of its body. We use a shape-memory alloy that changes shape when heated by a tiny laser, allowing the robot to propel itself forward.

W: So what real-world applications do you envision for such a tiny machine?

M: One major use is performing tasks in tightly confined spaces, like inside clogged arteries to clear blockages or assist in microsurgery. It could also explore structures too narrow for current tools. The goal is to eventually replace humans in some exploratory medical tasks, but for now, we're focused on confined-space operations.

W: Mark, I still can't believe you threw up on that bumpy bus ride to the coast! I just dozed off for perhaps ten minutes and woke up perfectly fine.
M: Well, you're lucky. Some people are genetically determined to need less sleep—they're called short sleepers. They can function well on just four or five hours.
W: So are they mostly immune to cognitive impairment? And can I somehow train myself to reach that elite status?
M: No, you can't modify your genes. It's innate. But remember, having a baby will severely disrupt your sleep patterns—new parents are forced to jump out of bed at the crack of dawn.
W: I see. Being forced to rise early is very different from being a natural early bird. I guess I'll just nurture my passion for sleep instead.
sectionC:
Good morning. Today we'll explore several fascinating findings that challenge common assumptions. First, consider a study on belief change. Researchers found that people often underestimate how much a single convincing article can sway their opinions. Interestingly, this effect seems to apply primarily to moderate beliefs—it may not hold for extreme or deeply held convictions. Our memories can also shift to align with our current beliefs, making us less aware of how much we've been influenced.

Next, let's look at trends in American motherhood. Over the past 25 years, fertility rates have actually been declining sharply, so American motherhood is on the decline. Yet simultaneously, a larger proportion of new mothers are economically able to raise children, and more women report truly enjoying motherhood. This suggests the meaning of motherhood has changed considerably—it's now more about choice and fulfillment than obligation.

Finally, a common belief is that houseplants improve indoor air quality. However, research indicates that in typical homes, houseplants have little to no effect on removing pollutants. NASA's famous experiments were conducted in sealed containers that resembled super-insulated offices from the 1970s, not normal environments. In reality, natural ventilation—simply opening windows—proves far more effective at cleaning the air than any number of houseplants. This case highlights the importance of continually reexamining scientific findings to avoid misinterpretations. We must be cautious about accepting popular conclusions without understanding the actual conditions of the experiments.

sectionA:
W: Thanks for meeting with me. I’ve reviewed your latest proposal.

M: Great. What do you think? We really need your reply within a short time – our client is getting impatient.

W: I understand. There’s one small issue. If we add a clause to prevent geographical discrimination, I can sign the agreement today.

M: That’s reasonable. We actually included that to avoid any conflict of interest across regions. So, you’re saying you’ll make the sponsorship deal for your client at this meeting?

W: Yes, that’s good news for you. But we’re also eyeing the food and beverage market in another area.

M: Which area? For us, that market is a potential for food and beverage, but we’ve heard real estate developers find it attractive too.

W: That’s true. However, my client sees it as very different from other markets. We need to be careful.

M: No problem. I’ll draw up the revised contract. Time is running out, so let’s finalize quickly.

W: Agreed.

W: Did you see the news? People are gathering on mountain tops all across North America. They're absolutely thrilled about the rare astronomical event happening tonight.

M: I know! It's a once-in-a-lifetime chance. This comet will come closest to Earth in more than one hundred years. That's why it's so formidable—the most impressive one we've seen in a century.

W: Exactly. Everyone's saying it won't just look like a bright star. It'll appear as a blur, like a fuzzy patch of light with a faint tail. Definitely not an ordinary flying object.

M: Right. So how can we get the best view? Do we need professional telescopes?

W: No, actually. You can just use a phone app to locate the comet. There are plenty of sky guide apps that show you exactly where to look. No need for expensive gear.

M: Perfect. Let's head up to the rooftop later and watch it together.
sectionB: There is growing debate among nutrition experts about whether consumers should be warned against ultra-processed foods. Some argue that current dietary guidelines lack sufficient scientific consensus to form a reliable basis for public advice. However, others believe that guidelines, even if imperfect, can still help consumers make informed choices. The main point of disagreement lies in how to define ultra-processed foods. While some classify them by the degree of industrial processing, others focus on the extent of chemical alteration or even labor cost. Yet a major concern is that many people misunderstand the nutritional value of these foods, which can lead to poor dietary habits. Additionally, studies have linked high consumption of ultra-processed foods to a greater risk of chronic diseases such as obesity and diabetes. This has prompted calls for clearer warning labels, but food scientists are divided on whether the concept itself is scientifically sound.

Today’s lecture explores how constraints influence creativity. Conventional wisdom suggests that unlimited freedom fosters innovation, but evidence points otherwise. When people encounter limitations, they often begin to see the world in a different way, shifting their perspective to uncover novel solutions. This adaptive response—turning a restriction into a springboard—is a defining trait of creative individuals. They treat limitation not as a barrier but as a catalyst. Consider resource scarcity: rather than stifling creativity, limited resources often motivate people to repurpose what is available, proving that abundance or scarcity has minimal bearing on original thinking. In organizational settings, well‑defined constraints do not hinder progress; instead, they shape and focus problems, making them more tractable. Ultimately, creativity does not demand absolute freedom—it thrives best when it is constrained. Limitations force deeper engagement, pushing us to explore paths we might otherwise overlook. Therefore, effective problem‑solving often begins by embracing the very boundaries we are tempted to resist.
sectionC:
Today we explore how conflict resolution, biodiversity crisis, and human identity converge on one insight: we are deeply interconnected. Effective conflict strategies are learned, not innate—the key is understanding the other side’s specific expectations. The wise approach, like the owl, values both goals and relationships, unlike the aggressive shark or avoidant turtle.

Now consider our planet. Earth has entered its sixth mass extinction, with species vanishing at alarming rates. Yet one project dwarfs all other conservation efforts: DNA barcoding. This international collaboration aims to catalogue every species, helping save them from extinction and boosting human health. It is the most exciting, timely, and inspirational scientific endeavour ever undertaken.

Finally, think of human individuality. The notion that we are separate, self-sufficient beings is a delusion—one rooted in the 17th century mindset, not reality. John Donne’s famous line “no man is an island” reminds us we are part of a bigger world. By embracing this awareness, we can see how disruptive our problems have become and work together persistently. Only then can we truly address global challenges.

sectionA:
W: Thanks for meeting with me, Mark. I wanted to discuss my annual performance review.

M: Of course, Lisa. Let's go through it. Overall, you're doing well, but we need to address a couple of issues the company faces regarding team communication.

W: That's fair. What bothers me most is that my contributions often go unrecognized. I feel the company culture overlooks individual merits.

M: I hear you. However, your supervisor noted that you can be too direct in emails. You might offend recipients. Try a softer approach.

W: So it's my simplistic way of dealing with others? I thought being frank saved time.

M: Sometimes it does, but we want to avoid misunderstanding. Now, on a brighter note, I know you're pursuing medicine. What's your ultimate goal?

W: I want to provide medical service to the community. My parents have always tried to create a positive learning environment for me, which fueled this dream.

M: Why internal medicine specifically?

W: It connects many other specialties with its broad coverage, allowing me to have a profound impact on a wide range of patients.

M: That's admirable. Keep up the great work.
sectionB:
Today I’d like to talk about the ongoing battle between security and usability in our digital lives. You’ve probably encountered those distorted letters or image puzzles when logging into a website—that’s a CAPTCHA. Its main goal is not to stop hackers from cracking your secret codes, as many assume, but rather to convince the computer that you are a real human being, not an automated bot. This helps prevent large-scale fake account creation or brute‑force attacks.

Now, what about the guidelines we’re told to follow—like using a mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols? These recommendations aim to increase password complexity, but ironically they often make passwords so hard to remember that people resort to writing them down or reusing them. So the real challenge isn’t to reduce the intricacy of the password itself; rather, it’s to help people manage their passwords effectively. That’s why many experts advocate for password managers or multi‑factor authentication.

Looking ahead, simply providing incentives for creative passwords won’t solve the root problem. Instead of asking users to come up with ever more complicated secret codes, we should innovate technologies that ensure account safety without burdening the user. Biometrics, behavioral analysis, and one‑time codes are promising directions. The goal is to make security seamless so that the user is protected without even thinking about it.

W: When I first launched my fitness app, I was so excited. But after a year, I almost shut it down. My business was losing money, and I felt like giving up.

M: What made you change your mind?

W: I realized the core issue wasn’t the app itself—it was my approach. I redesigned it to focus on what users really needed: an AI that could assess their current fitness levels and create fully personalized training programs. No more generic workouts.

M: So the AI does everything?

W: Not quite. The workouts it generates are never repeated, which keeps my clients engaged. That variety is what helps them stay focused on their long-term goals rather than getting bored.

M: But can AI really replace a human trainer?

W: That’s the big question. While the AI’s mathematical models are efficient, they struggle to interpret the subtle details users put into their profiles—like emotional states or past injuries. So they can’t yet match a human coach’s intuition for arranging truly meticulous schedules. That’s the limitation we’re still working on.
sectionC:
Today I’d like to discuss several studies on morality, sensory experience, and well-being. First, consider moral rules. Researchers have long debated whether all societies share a common moral foundation. Some argue that seven basic moral rules, such as fairness and loyalty, are universal. To test this, a study examined whether deference to authority is unique to right-wing individuals or appears across groups. They found that even left-wing participants showed group loyalty, suggesting morality is more complex. To truly understand, we need systematic field studies to gather new data, rather than relying solely on existing descriptions.

Next, let’s turn to eating. Our senses, especially vision and smell, play a crucial role. They can be considered windows to our soul, directly activating brain functions. Food companies increasingly attend to how presentation affects taste. Actually, the visual aspect can change the way we perceive flavors. But more importantly, context and enrichment enhance the overall eating experience.

Finally, meaning and happiness. Many seek happiness, but recent research focuses on meaning. Social status and financial resources have limited effect; instead, meaning is key. One study found that the influence of these factors on happiness varies with social contexts. Using data from multiple countries, they discovered that people in individualistic societies might focus on personal satisfaction, and thus be less adversely impacted by failing to achieve a collective purpose.

sectionB: Good afternoon. I'm Dr. Evans from the Child Development Institute. Today I want to discuss a large-scale study on television and young children. The findings indicate that daily TV viewing significantly reduces the amount of time parents and children spend talking to each other. This reduction in conversation, rather than the TV content itself, was associated with slower development in reading and math skills. Interestingly, the study did not find that TV affected children's curiosity. Instead, the biggest concern was that TV consumed time that children could have used for hands-on exploratory activities. Such activities are vital for behavioral development, including learning to interact with peers. The study also noted that children from lower socioeconomic families were more affected, widening the gap in school readiness. Therefore, while some educational TV can be beneficial, parents should be mindful that excessive viewing can hinder important developmental interactions.

In today's lecture, we explore the pitfalls of modern ambition. Many people fall into the trap of what we call "mindless acquisition"—being driven not by purpose, but by an endless desire for more material possessions. This constant chase often isolates us from our fellow beings, as we become so focused on personal gain that we neglect relationships and community. The real danger is not just loneliness; it's developing an incorrect sense of well-being, mistaking wealth for happiness.

So how should we set our life goals? Contrary to popular belief, they shouldn't be based solely on theoretical concepts or even personal interests. True fulfillment comes when we include goals that help other people. This outward focus increases our sense of worthiness and connects us to something larger than ourselves.

Finally, what is the worst outcome of a misguided life? Perhaps it is not giving up the chance to fulfill yourself, but something more subtle—drifting through life aimlessly. Without clear, meaningful goals, we abandon all that life has to offer, and eventually, we risk spoiling our character and integrity. So ask yourself: Are you driving your life, or are you just being driven?
sectionA:
W: Hey John, I heard you’re thinking about switching from Economics to Art History. Is that true?
M: Yeah, I’ve been considering it. My family doesn’t really support it – they worry about my future.
W: But you’re passionate about art. Why not follow that?
M: Passion isn’t enough. I don’t think Art History will provide me with a living. It seems like a field for people who already have financial security.
W: Fair point. So what’s your plan?
M: I want to find a balance – maybe work in a related field that pays while still pursuing art on the side. That’s why for our end-of-semester business project, I chose to analyze the beef industry. It’s practical.
W: Interesting. Your group is looking at beef consumption? How does that work?
M: We’re studying whether the country should raise cattle domestically instead of importing most of the beef. It affects economic growth and farmland expansion.
W: Sounds like a solid project. What about the other group – the one with the business consultancy?
M: They’re advising a carwash startup targeting high-end customers in big cities, not just any car owners. The clients want professional service and a good markup.
W: So technical equipment isn’t their focus?
M: No, it’s more about positioning and pricing. I think their approach is smart.
sectionC: Good morning. Today we'll examine three common misconceptions. First, regarding exam preparation: many students seek grade-raising ways, but research shows that brain-boosting food and drink such as dark chocolate and green tea can enhance concentration. However, avoid consuming coffee after 2 pm to prevent sleep disruption. Also, instead of taking varied vitamin supplements, it's better to consume a rich variety of foods for optimal nutrition. Second, consider global economic perceptions. Surprisingly, many people across advanced economies think the world is going from bad to worse, despite data showing that globalization has experienced ups and downs like any historical trend. The reality is that while many countries have thrived, individual fortunes may take a downturn in this volatile economy. Third, let's discuss why smells activate emotional memories. The scent particles travel directly to the brain's emotional and memory centers, bypassing other processing areas. This explains why a familiar scent can vividly revive long‑forgotten memories. However, these olfactory memories are often inaccurate and alterable over time. So, next time you catch a whiff of bread, remember your brain is playing tricks.

sectionB: Today, I’d like to discuss a fascinating study that sheds light on how aging affects memory and creativity. The researchers investigated the relationship between prior knowledge and memory retrieval, proposing an explanation for why older adults often struggle to recall specific information. They found that older adults tend to retrieve irrelevant memories alongside the ones they want, leading to cluttered recollections. This is because their extensive prior knowledge creates a dense network of associations, making it harder to isolate a single memory. However, the study also revealed a surprising benefit: older adults show preserved, and sometimes even enhanced, creativity compared to younger individuals. Their ability to draw from a wider range of experiences allows for more novel connections. In contrast, young adults rely more heavily on memory alone, without the same breadth of accumulated knowledge. So while aging may slow down precise memory retrieval, it can foster creative thinking that draws on a lifetime of learning. This insight challenges the common view that cognitive decline is simply a loss of function.

In today’s lecture, we’ll explore the paradox of modern work. Many employees take pride in the goods and services they produce, yet they often feel they have little control over their daily tasks. This lack of autonomy contributes to a growing sense of being time-poor—a feeling that there are never enough hours in the day. Studies show this time scarcity is more damaging to well-being than even rising economic inequality or declining productivity. Meanwhile, technological advancements are reshaping the workplace. While some fear that automation puts jobs and wages at risk, others argue it liberates people from tedious labor and creates new opportunities in IT. But the deeper question remains: how do we find meaning in work? Is it through pursuing purpose in our careers, or by prioritizing life outside the office? Perhaps the key lies not in the workplace alone but in strengthening community relationships and realizing our social value beyond the job. These are the tensions we must address as we rethink the future of work.
sectionA:
W: I'm so frustrated! My computer crashed this morning, and I couldn't get any work done. Did you manage to call anyone?

M: Actually, I already contacted Alpha Maintenance. They said they'd send someone over. But what did you do first?

W: I consulted someone in charge at the IT department. They told me to inform the central office immediately, so I did. Then they designated an engineer to the case. But the engineer hasn't arrived yet.

M: That's annoying. Before we do anything drastic, we need to check one thing — whether all the contents have been backed up. If they have, we can just restore everything. If not, we might have lost all data on the hard drive.

W: I really hope it's backed up. Anyway, let's change the subject. You've been working in property law for a while now. How do you find it?

M: It's challenging, but I actually love it. Most people think it's boring, but for me it's a beautiful thing — unlike most jobs, it forces you to think on your feet. My main role is mediating land disputes and buying and selling property, but sometimes I also arbitrate between disagreeing solicitors. The goal is always to prevent disputes from escalating.

W: Do you ever have to go to court?

M: Rarely. Courts can be frustrating and expensive, and they're really intended for fixing major legal disputes. So we try to avoid them. What really matters in this job is the skill of preventing conflicts between parties concerned, along with the ability to express themselves clearly and forcefully. That's what makes a good lawyer.
sectionC:
Today I'd like to discuss three seemingly unrelated topics that share a common thread: social intelligence. First, consider a study where infants watched an adult push a big button on top of a box. The babies imitated the action, but only when the adult pressed it intentionally. This shows social intelligence may outweigh IQ in importance and affects our attitude to novel tasks.

Next, let's talk about punctuality. Many people struggle with habitual lateness. A simple solution is to get prepared the night before and to finish any prior task thirty minutes earlier than necessary. This leaves buffer time and helps avoid hold-ups.

Finally, cultural differences in social behavior are striking. For instance, traits such as willingness to help strangers vary widely. In some cities, people are not as willing to assist others. Boston, however, has a unique tradition of courtesy that was adopted by its upper class. Proper Bostonians do not see it as exotic; they prefer to stick to their own way of showing courtesy rather than follow examples from places like New York or Paris.

sectionA:
W: We really need to decide by the end of the day. Both Rachel and Peter are extremely keen on the job, but our team is split on the procedure.

M: I agree. They’re both competitive candidates. Rachel and Peter came across as respectful and professional in their interviews. Rachel’s grades were moderately higher, but both excelled academically. Peter seemed slightly stronger physically.

W: True. But what stood out to me was Peter’s use of body language—very confident. His experience is solid too, though his state of mind seemed a bit unusual under pressure.

M: So how do we break the tie? Should we compare them side by side again? Or ask the board to cast the deciding vote?

W: I think we need to find a way to break the tie this time. Maybe let John make the final decision—he knows both from past projects.

W: I'm so glad you could come see my latest collection. It's a bit different from what I've done before.

M: I've been looking forward to it. Your private label "L’Orange" always pushes boundaries. What's the concept this season?

W: I wanted to create something slightly absurd — a playful departure from the usual social norms and public expectations. Fashion can be too predictable.

M: Absurd? That sounds intriguing. How did you achieve that in your designs?

W: I merge styles that are often at opposite ends of the fashion spectrum. For example, I take a traditional power suit and draw intricate patterns inspired by Asian rural mountain villagers onto it. Then I pair it with a velvet cape that looks both majestic and masculine — it's a clash that somehow works.

M: That's brilliant. I've been trying to incorporate indigenous aspects into my own work, but never thought to combine them with such contrasting elements.

W: It's all about breaking the rules while keeping a sense of coherence. You should see the full collection — there's a lot more variety.

M: I'd love to. Actually, that's exactly what I was hoping for — seeing your new creations in person. And maybe we can share more ideas next time.

W: Absolutely. Let's schedule a studio visit.
sectionB: Today, I’d like to discuss a fascinating study on dog cognition. Researchers found that dogs can follow human pointing gestures without any prior training—a skill not seen in wolves, even if raised by humans. This ability suggests that domestication has uniquely shaped dogs’ social intelligence. The same study showed that dogs do not necessarily recognize their owner just by looking; they rely more on cues like voice or scent. While dogs can understand some commands, this does not imply they grasp the underlying implications. The key insight is that understanding how humans impact their behavior is crucial for improving our relationship with them. Rather than trying to cultivate them into intelligent creatures through force, or training them to behave like domesticated dogs in rigid ways, we should focus on treating them with sensitivity and respect. This approach acknowledges their natural social skills and enhances cooperation. So, instead of merely taming through repetition or forcing responsiveness, observe their behavior and adapt to their needs.

Today we're going to talk about how to organize your refrigerator efficiently. Most people assume the temperature is uniform, but in fact, the temperature inside it varies from place to place. The back of the bottom shelf is the coldest area, while the doors are the warmest. This means different shelves are designed for different purposes. For instance, the top shelf is best for drinks and leftovers, the middle section for dairy and eggs, and the bottom shelf for raw meat. Proper placement facilitates access to food products and ensures they stay fresh longer. One key purpose of proper fridge organization is to slow the rising of temperature inside when you open the door. This helps prevent germs from growing quickly and keeps food cold as long as possible. Where should you store items like milk? Most experts recommend putting them in the middle section, not on the door, because the door's temperature fluctuates too much. If you place delicate vegetables at the back of the bottom shelf, they will become extra-chilly and may even be ruined by freezing. So remember: understand your fridge's temperature zones and arrange food accordingly to avoid spoilage and maximize freshness.
sectionC: In a recent lecture on aging and well-being, the speaker highlighted that chronic depression often triggers a cascade of physical decline, including growing feebleness and a higher risk of hip fracture, though fatal infections remain the leading cause of death in the very old. Turning to a research project at the University of Birmingham, the lecturer explained that its success comes from combining multiple perspectives—bringing together experts from different age groups and disciplines—which makes the findings feel real and relevant, outperforming much academic work. Shifting focus to culinary education, the speaker noted that many cooking schools fail because they don’t teach basic organization; they emphasize techniques and recipes but overlook how to manage a kitchen efficiently. Celebrity chefs, however, view cooking as a way of transformation, not just a job. The lecture then drew on a philosopher’s advice: use time and resources wisely, and cultivate self-discipline, as respect follows. Finally, discussing motivation, the speaker argued that the basis for self-improvement is the reason for stagnation, not just hard work. When we feel we are always falling behind others, we lose confidence and satisfaction. The key is not measuring against the gap but measuring where we’ve come from—seeing the gain, not just the goal. This shift in perspective helps us notice our progress and stop imagining that life goals are ever fully achieved.

sectionB: In workplace conflicts, what we call a "he-said-she-said" situation often arises when two people tell entirely different stories about the same event. This is not merely when they behave in a clashing manner or when their managers direct them; it is fundamentally a case of conflicting narratives. When such a situation occurs, managers typically focus not on how the dispute got resolved or when it was settled, but on how it happened. Their primary reaction is not to intervene promptly to prevent escalation or to persuade the parties to put aside their conflict. Instead, they try to ascertain the actual facts of the matter, carefully examining evidence and interviewing witnesses to determine what truly took place. Only after establishing the facts can they take appropriate action.

W: I've been trying to change my spending habits lately. Eating out every day just isn't realistic anymore.

M: I know what you mean. I've started budgeting sensibly and cooking at home more. It really helps me keep track of my money.

W: That's smart. I've also taken up a new hobby—sewing. It's surprisingly relaxing, and I can modify old clothes instead of buying new ones.

M: Great idea. A lot of people are shifting their focus. Instead of always looking for convenience, they're finding ways to cut down on unnecessary expenses.

W: Exactly. It's not that we hate cooking or anything. But with rising costs, we have to be more careful. Even grocery shopping has become a planned activity.

M: Right. I think many are realizing that cooking at home isn't just about taste—it's about saving money and eating better too.

W: And sewing saves me a fortune on fashion. I'd rather spend time making something unique than shopping mindlessly.

M: So overall, people are becoming more cost-conscious. The old obsession with convenience is fading as finances get tighter.

W: True. I don't dine out daily anymore, and I actually enjoy the process of preparing meals. It's become a new normal for us.
sectionC:
Today we’ll explore three topics that frequently puzzle travelers: lost luggage, tourist behavior, and alternative accommodations. First, why does luggage go missing? It may happen when your flight is delayed due to bad weather, or when airlines cannot identify the owner because the bag lacks an ID tag.
